app.name: Eraser (DiagramGPT)
app.bestFor: The top pick for technical diagrams — architecture, system design, ERDs and sequence flows generated from a text description.
app.rankBasis: Placed #1 in both Dupple's tested 2026 list and Toolradar's 2026 ranking, and appears in all three cited roundups; reviewers consistently single out its architecture, ER and sequence-diagram focus over general whiteboards.
app.pricingFree: Free: 3 files, 3 AI credits, 7-day version history, unlimited guests
app.pricingPaid: Starter $15/member/mo billed annually ($20 monthly) — unlimited files, 40 AI credits and 90-day version history
features: editable diagram-as-code on an infinite canvas
GitHub, Notion, Confluence and VS Code integrations
pros: reviewers rate it the strongest specialist for technical and architecture diagrams
keeps diagrams, Markdown notes and version-controlled source in one engineering workspace
3,000+ technical icons and editable AI output reduce cleanup for system designs
cons: the 3-file free cap is restrictive for ongoing work
40 monthly AI credits on Starter can run out quickly during iteration
technical focus and diagram-as-code workflow are less suitable for casual business diagrams

app.name: Mermaid Chart
app.bestFor: AI-generated diagram-as-code you can version-control, with the widest integration support of any option.
app.rankBasis: Placed #4 in Dupple's tested 2026 list and #2 in ScreenApp's developer-focused review, appears in all three cited roundups, and builds on Mermaid's ~89.4k-star ecosystem plus native GitHub rendering.
app.pricingFree: Basic: 3 diagrams, limited diagram size, 15 AI credits and 1 team/project
app.pricingPaid: Plus €8/user/mo billed annually — unlimited diagrams, 300 AI credits/year and view/comment collaboration
pros: diagram source is plain text that works well in Git and documentation reviews
inherits Mermaid's broad diagram-type and integration ecosystem
AI lowers the syntax barrier while keeping the output editable
cons: the Basic plan's diagram and AI limits are tight
complex diagrams still require Mermaid syntax knowledge and manual cleanup
automatic layout offers less precise placement than a full canvas editor

app.name: Lucidchart
app.bestFor: Broadest diagram-type coverage on an editable, collaborative canvas — strongest for enterprise, though more template- than AI-driven.
app.rankBasis: Appears in all three cited 2026 roundups; Toolradar reports 4.5/5 on G2 from 8,430 reviews and 4.5/5 on Capterra from 2,244, the deepest independent review base in this list.
app.pricingFree: Free: 3 editable documents, 75 shapes/document and 100 templates
app.pricingPaid: Individual plan — unlimited editable documents and objects, 1 GB storage and Visio import/export; price is shown during checkout
features: flowcharts, UML, ER, network and org diagrams
real-time co-editing, comments and presentation mode
Lucid AI generation and formatting assistance
pros: large template and shape library covers far more diagram types than specialist AI tools
mature collaboration and enterprise administration are repeatedly praised
approachable canvas works for technical and non-technical teammates
cons: per-user pricing adds up for larger teams
the free plan's 3-document and 75-shape limits are restrictive
reviewers find its AI less architecture-specific than Eraser and the app heavy for quick sketches

app.name: Whimsical AI
app.bestFor: Fast, beautiful flowcharts and mind maps — the speed-and-aesthetics winner for lighter-weight diagramming.
app.rankBasis: Placed #2 in Toolradar's 2026 ranking with a reported 4.6/5 G2 rating from 188 reviews and #3 in Dupple's tested list; it appears in both cited roundups for polished flowcharts.
app.pricingFree: Free: 3 team boards, 3 teams, 10 guests, 7-day history and 20 AI credits/workspace total
app.pricingPaid: Pro $10/editor/mo billed annually — unlimited team boards, 50 guests, 1 GB uploads and 90-day history
features: diagrams, wireframes and documents on one canvas
pros: reviewers consistently praise its clean interface and minimal learning curve
AI output is among the most polished and presentation-ready
fast flowchart, wireframe and product-planning workflow
cons: limited architecture-specific shapes and formal UML/ER support
20 free AI credits are a one-time workspace allowance, not monthly
advanced security, unlimited boards and longer history require paid tiers

app.name: Miro AI
app.bestFor: Collaborative design sessions on an infinite canvas, best when a team is diagramming together in real time.
app.rankBasis: Appears in both cited 2026 roundups as the collaboration-first option; Miro reports 20,000+ aggregate reviews across G2, Capterra and TrustRadius, far more community signal than newer AI-native entrants.
app.pricingFree: Free: 3 editable boards, 7,000+ templates, 250+ integrations and limited AI trial usage
app.pricingPaid: Starter $8/member/mo billed annually — unlimited private boards, 25 AI credits/member/mo and 500 MCP calls/day
features: Mermaid, UML, AWS and process-diagram shapes
pros: real-time workshops and cross-functional collaboration are its clearest review strength
large template, shape and integration ecosystem
AI can turn brainstorm content into diagrams without leaving the shared board
cons: reviewers describe the broad interface as overwhelming for diagram-only use
large boards can become cluttered and harder to navigate
many technical-system features and higher AI allowances sit above Starter

app.name: Napkin AI
app.bestFor: Turning written text into clean presentation-ready visuals rather than rigorous engineering diagrams.
app.rankBasis: Ranked #2 in Dupple's tested 2026 list for text-to-visual work and highlighted in independent comparisons for design-forward output; it ranks lower here because reviewers say technical structures are not guaranteed.
app.pricingFree: Free: 500 AI credits/week, unlimited visual editing and Napkin branding on exports
app.pricingPaid: Plus $9/person/mo billed annually ($12 monthly) — 10,000 AI credits/mo and unbranded visuals
pros: reviewers praise how quickly prose becomes presentation-ready visuals
multiple generated options make visual iteration easy
friendly for non-designers and non-technical users
cons: technical diagram structures and notation are not guaranteed
less precise layout and relationship control than architecture-specific tools
free exports retain Napkin branding

app.name: InfraSketch
app.bestFor: Purpose-built for software architecture, using Claude to turn system requirements into complete architecture diagrams.
app.rankBasis: The only cited comparison is InfraSketch's own vendor-authored test and independent review volume remains sparse, so it ranks seventh despite a differentiated architecture-diagram plus design-document workflow.
app.pricingFree: Free: 10 credits/mo; diagram generation costs 5–15 credits
app.pricingPaid: Starter $1/mo — 50 credits/mo plus design-document generation and export
pros: purpose-built around software architecture rather than generic whiteboarding
combines diagrams and design-document generation in one workflow
published entry pricing is unusually low
cons: very limited independent review and community validation
the 10-credit free allowance may not cover one maximum-cost 15-credit generation
the vendor says real-time collaboration is not yet available

skill.name: Mermaid
skill.bestFor: The de-facto text-to-diagram standard (~89.4k★) — natively rendered in GitHub/GitLab Markdown, making it the default diagram-as-code format agents produce for architecture, flow and sequence diagrams.
skill.rankBasis: The default diagram-as-code standard at ~89.4k GitHub stars, native rendering in GitHub/GitLab and most documentation platforms, and the #1 recommendation in 2026 diagram-as-code comparisons.
pros: largest ecosystem and widest native integration support in diagram-as-code
plain-text diagrams version and review cleanly with source code
low barrier for common flow, sequence and architecture diagrams
cons: layout control is limited and nodes cannot be positioned precisely
large diagrams become difficult to read in source and rendered form
syntax mistakes can break rendering and architecture-specific icon support is limited

skill.name: Excalidraw
skill.bestFor: The dominant open-source virtual whiteboard (~128.3k★) — hand-drawn aesthetic, hugely used for freeform architecture sketches and system diagrams.
skill.rankBasis: The most-starred project here at ~128.3k GitHub stars and #4 in Dupple's tested 2026 diagram-tool list; it ranks second because its adoption is for freeform sketching rather than structured diagram-as-code.
pros: reviewers repeatedly praise its instant start and near-zero learning curve
hand-drawn style works especially well for early architecture discussion
free, local-first and easy to share for collaborative sketches
cons: manual layout and freeform shapes are weak for formal UML or precise documentation
smaller structured shape library than dedicated architecture tools
complex drawings require manual organization and are harder to maintain as living docs

skill.name: Diagrams (mingrammer)
skill.bestFor: The leading diagram-as-code library for cloud/system architecture (~42.5k★) — draw AWS/GCP/Azure/Kubernetes architecture from Python.
skill.rankBasis: The leading Python cloud-architecture library at ~42.5k GitHub stars and the dedicated Python pick in 2026 diagram-as-code comparisons, with documented use in projects such as Apache Airflow.
features: AWS, Azure, GCP and Kubernetes icon sets
pros: excellent provider-icon coverage for cloud and infrastructure diagrams
Python makes diagrams programmable from configuration or loops
diagram source versions cleanly beside infrastructure code
cons: requires Python plus a separate Graphviz installation
limited to architecture/infrastructure rather than sequence, ER or general flow diagrams
automatic layout offers little precise node placement or interactive editing

skill.name: D2
skill.bestFor: Fast-rising modern diagram scripting language (~24.8k★) — cleaner syntax than PlantUML with strong automatic layout.
skill.rankBasis: A fast-growing modern DSL at ~24.8k GitHub stars and the #2 recommendation in 2026 diagram-as-code comparisons, behind Mermaid mainly because its ecosystem and native integrations are smaller.
features: Dagre, ELK and optional TALA layouts
pros: clean syntax and attractive defaults are repeatedly preferred over older DSLs
multiple layout engines provide more control than Mermaid
nested containers work well for grouped architecture views
cons: smaller integration and documentation ecosystem than Mermaid
no native GitHub rendering, so publishing needs a build step
the TALA layout engine requires a separate paid license

skill.name: tldraw
skill.bestFor: Infinite-canvas whiteboard SDK (~49.2k★) — the toolkit developers build collaborative diagramming apps on.
skill.rankBasis: Strong developer adoption at ~49.2k GitHub stars and a polished canvas SDK, but it ranks fifth because it is a source-available application toolkit rather than an out-of-box architecture-diagram format.
skill.pricingFree: Free for development; free 100-day production trial and discretionary hobby license
skill.pricingPaid: Commercial production license with value-based annual pricing via sales; no public rate
pros: widely adopted, polished foundation for building custom collaborative diagram apps
deep customization across shapes, tools, storage and interface
active project with strong developer documentation
cons: the SDK is source-available, not permissively open-source
production use requires a license key after the 100-day trial
developers must build the architecture semantics, icons and workflows themselves

skill.name: PlantUML
skill.bestFor: The long-standing text-to-UML workhorse (~13.2k★) — most complete UML coverage, ubiquitous in enterprise toolchains despite dated visuals.
skill.rankBasis: The long-standing UML workhorse at ~13.2k GitHub stars, ranked #3 in 2026 diagram-as-code comparisons and backed by decades of enterprise integrations despite a smaller GitHub footprint.
features: sequence, component, deployment and activity diagrams
large cloud and technology icon libraries
pros: broadest mature UML coverage among text-based tools
battle-tested integrations across enterprise IDEs and documentation systems
extensive standard-library icons for technical architecture
cons: verbose syntax has a steeper learning curve than Mermaid or D2
local rendering requires Java or a PlantUML server
automatic layouts can be awkward and default visuals look dated

skill.name: C4-PlantUML
skill.bestFor: The standard way to render C4-model software architecture diagrams as code (~7.4k★) — the go-to for C4 + text.
skill.rankBasis: The de-facto C4 extension for PlantUML at ~7.4k GitHub stars, with dedicated macros and examples for every C4 level; it ranks below general tools because its scope is intentionally architecture-model specific.
features: C4 context, container and component diagrams
layout macros, tags and custom styling
VS Code snippets and PlantUML compatibility
pros: well-established text workflow for teams already using the C4 model
consistent notation across multiple architecture abstraction levels
inherits PlantUML's rendering and integration ecosystem
cons: requires PlantUML and its Java/server toolchain
narrower use case than general diagramming libraries
layout macros and version compatibility can require manual tuning

skill.name: draw.io (drawio)
skill.bestFor: The client-side GUI diagram editor behind diagrams.net (~7k★ source repo, but product usage is enormous) — broad diagram-type coverage with no lock-in.
skill.rankBasis: Dupple ranks diagrams.net #2 among tested 2026 diagram tools and calls it the strongest fully free option; the source repo has ~7k stars, while product adoption is much larger than that repo count suggests.
features: web, desktop and self-hosted diagram editor
PNG, SVG, PDF and XML export
pros: fully featured with no account, document cap or core paywall
broad architecture and business diagram coverage
offline use and portable XML files avoid vendor lock-in
cons: reviewers commonly describe the interface as dated and less polished
real-time collaboration is not available in the open-source editor
no native AI generation and source XML is less readable than text DSLs

skill.name: Mermaid Diagrams (softaworks agent-toolkit)
skill.bestFor: The most-adopted diagram Claude Code skill (~2.2k★, whole toolkit) — a mermaid-diagrams SKILL.md for flowcharts, sequence and architecture diagrams.
skill.rankBasis: The parent toolkit has ~2.2k GitHub stars, the strongest adoption signal among the two dedicated agent skills here, but there is no skill-specific star count or meaningful independent review base.
features: architecture, C4, ER and sequence references
pros: broad reference material covers the Mermaid diagrams software teams use most
clear selection guidance and examples help agents choose an appropriate diagram type
portable Agent Skills packaging works across multiple coding agents
cons: the ~2.2k-star signal belongs to the whole toolkit, not this skill
no automated syntax validation or render-and-review loop is bundled
independent user reviews and skill-specific adoption data are sparse

skill.name: Mermaid Skill (Agents365-ai)
skill.bestFor: The most complete standalone Mermaid skill (~146★) — natural language to validated .mmd with 11+ diagram types and mmdc/Kroki rendering to PNG/SVG/PDF.
skill.rankBasis: A newer standalone skill at ~146 GitHub stars with 11+ diagram types and a documented validation/export loop; it ranks tenth because adoption and independent community feedback remain small.
features: natural language to validated Mermaid source
pros: validation-first workflow catches syntax errors before export
supports local mmdc rendering with a zero-install Kroki fallback
adds visual review and iterative cleanup beyond basic Mermaid generation
cons: small ~146-star adoption base and almost no independent review signal
rendering depends on an external mmdc installation or Kroki service
limited to Mermaid rather than supporting multiple diagram engines

notes: Skill/Repo track rebuilt to rank by real adoption, not form factor. The recognized OSS diagramming tools lead as kind:repo — Mermaid is placed #1 as the de-facto diagram-as-code / architecture standard (natively rendered on GitHub and what agents emit), ahead of the higher-starred but freeform Excalidraw whiteboard — followed by the diagram-as-code leaders (Diagrams, D2, PlantUML, C4-PlantUML, draw.io). Genuine diagram Claude skills are a young, entirely Mermaid-centric space topping out in the low thousands of stars, so softaworks' toolkit and the standalone Agents365 skill rank below the major tools. Removed all MCP-server entries (Mermaid MCP, Claude Mermaid, Whimsical MCP). Star counts verified live 2026-07-24.
